channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Amid War, Iran’s Soccer Leader Works to Get His Team to the World Cup
item.description: Mehdi Taj said his federation has been dealing with FIFA, not the United States, in trying to obtain visas for Iran to play in the tournament that begins next week.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mehdi Taj being interviewed by a reporter in Tehran last month. Mr. Taj said mixed messages from Washington had created confusion over Iran’s participation in the World Cup.

item.title: Trump Agitates Brazil’s Politics, Again
item.description: President Trump appears ready to lend a hand to a new right-wing ally ahead of a crucial election this year that will be the biggest test for Latin America’s left.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Adriano Machado/Reuters
item.media:description: Flávio Bolsonaro in Brasília following his meeting with President Trump at the White House.

item.title: Trump Endorses Abelardo De La Espriella, Right-Wing Presidential Candidate in Colombia
item.description: Mr. Trump said that the candidate, Abelardo De La Espriella, was important for the U.S.-Colombia relationship and called his left-wing rival a “Radical Left Marxist.”
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella at his closing rally in Medellín, Colombia. He gave campaign speeches from a bulletproof booth.

item.title: Toddler Dies After Bouncy Castle Is Lofted by Harsh Winds in Montreal
item.description: Severe thunderstorms lashed an annual church party, sending airborne an inflatable toy often found at outdoor parties, paramedics said.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai

item.title: Trump Again Refers to Canada as the ‘51st State’ Hours Before Trade Talks
item.description: The Canadian trade minister met with the U.S. Trade Representative on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Yuri Cortez/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Dominic LeBlanc, Canada’s minister in charge of trade, is scheduled to meet with Jamieson Greer, the United States Trade Representative, in Washington on Tuesday.

item.title: Carney Says Canada Faces a ‘Crisis of Antisemitism’
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s comments followed calls for action from Jewish organizations and after a government report found that antisemitism had surged in Canada in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking at Holy Blossom Temple in Toronto on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Was Asked to Blacklist Colombian Cartel Gold. It Was Also Buying It.
item.description: The request for sanctions, from Colombia’s defense minister, shows failures at both ends of the supply chain for illegal gold.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pedro Sánchez, center, Colombia’s defense minister, visiting an illegal mining operation in and around a military base last month.

item.title: A Stray Dog Became a Cultural Symbol in Brazil. Now, Mexico Is Claiming It.
item.description: To Brazilians, the ubiquitous caramel-colored mutts are a national icon. But a Mexican state has adopted them as its own homegrown symbol.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and María Magdalena Arréllaga
item.media:description: Tiana, a “caramelo,” at a park in Rio de Janeiro in April.

item.title: Trump Targets Brazil With 25% Tariff, Citing Unfair Trade Practices
item.description: The proposal marks the latest effort by the administration to rebuild its tariff agenda through Section 301 investigations.
item.dc:creator: Daisuke Wakabayashi
item.media:credit: Rodolfo Buhrer/Reuters
item.media:description: Bulk carriers at the port of Paranagua, Brazil, in March.

item.title: Trump Stands to Gain a Key Ally in Colombia’s Upcoming Election
item.description: Abelardo De La Espriella, a hard-line candidate on the right facing a left-wing rival in a presidential runoff, has pledged to crush drug traffickers.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella, at a campaign event in Medellín in May, captured more than 40 percent of the vote on Sunday.

item.title: Alberta’s Movement to Separate from Canada Gets Its Moment
item.description: In October, Albertans will get to say if they want to stay in Canada, or hold a referendum to leave. Will it settle the matter, or deepen the rift?
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Amber Bracken
item.media:description: A table set up to gather signatures for a separation petition along a road in Calgary, Alberta, in February.

item.title: An Industrial Gem in Venezuela Now Embodies the Country’s Decay
item.description: Cumaná was once an economic hub, producing Toyota Land Cruisers and exporting food across South America. Now it’s on the brink of disaster as public services collapse.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:description: Gathering water at a stream in Cumaná.

item.title: What We Saw Driving Through Venezuela
item.description: Our reporter Simon Romero takes a roadtrip from Caracas to Cumaná in Venezuela, showing the multiple military checkpoints the regime has along the coast, a constant reminder of the state’s presence.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero, Leila Medina, Thomas Vollkommer, Joey Sendaydiego and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez

item.title: Brooklyn Rivera, Nicaraguan Indigenous Leader, Dies in Government Custody
item.description: Brooklyn Rivera, 73, one of the most prominent Miskito political leaders in recent history, was arrested by the government in 2023. His family and other groups dispute the circumstances surrounding his death.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Lou Dematteis/Reuters
item.media:description: The Miskito rebel leader Brooklyn Rivera, right, speaking during a news conference in Managua, Nicaragua, as his fellow leader Steadman Fagoth Müller looked on, in 1988.

item.title: Colombia Presidential Election Heads to a Runoff
item.description: The candidate, Abelardo de la Espriella, will face a senator from the left-wing party of the departing president, Gustavo Petro, in a June runoff.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Genevieve Glatsky and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.media:credit: Sergio Acero/Reuters
item.media:description: Presidential candidate Abelardo De La Espriella addressing supporters in Barranquilla, Colombia, on Sunday.

item.title: Even Havana’s Street Sweepers Can’t Escape the Impact of the Fuel Blockade
item.description: A fuel blockade on Cuba has aggravated a lack of consistent garbage pickup and produced enormous trash piles.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Yamil Lage/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Garbage on the street in Havana in December.

item.title: As Deaths From U.S. Boat Strikes Pass 200, Locals Tally an Even Greater Cost
item.description: Residents of coastal communities in Colombia and Ecuador said the airstrike campaign was making many reconsider anything involving the ocean as a livelihood.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and José María León Cabrera
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The remains of a burned boat near Puerto López, Guajira, Colombia in December.

item.title: Where Is Toronto’s Best Party? Try the Basement.
item.description: Millennials who yearn for a bygone era of dancing that feels pure and more organic are bringing back the basement jam, popularized decades ago by Toronto’s Caribbean diaspora.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Ian Willms
item.media:description: Unlike most nightclubs, the Caribbean-style basement jams don’t adhere to strict dress-codes, and there are no private booths. The parties hark back to a simpler time.

item.title: Is a Canadian Car the Answer to Trump’s Tariffs? The Bricklin Shows the Risks.
item.description: A sports car made in New Brunswick and mostly funded by the province lasted only about 18 months.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: The Provincial Archives of New Brunswick
item.media:description: The businessman Malcolm Bricklin and Premier Richard Hatfield at the Bricklin factory in Saint John, New Brunswick. The company’s sports car was funded by the province.

item.title: Colombia’s Elections Are a Crucial Test for the Left in Latin America
item.description: Colombia’s leftist candidate for president leads in polls, but his main rival — a far-right outsider who calls himself “The Tiger” — has gained momentum.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo De La Espriella, a far-right candidate for president of Colombia, closed his campaign in the city of Medellín.

item.title: Canadian Man Pleads Guilty to Aiding 14 Suicides
item.description: Kenneth Law, who ran an online business that shipped toxic salt to customers in 40 countries, also admitted to causing the deaths of 79 people in Britain, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Arlyn Mcadorey/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Kenneth Law, a Canadian man accused of running businesses that shipped toxic salt to people in dozens of countries, pleaded guilty on Friday to 14 counts of aiding suicide in Ontario, prosecutors said.

item.title: Mexican Senate Votes to Allow Voiding Elections Over Foreign Interference
item.description: The legislation, which comes amid growing tensions between Mexico and the White House, must be approved by a majority of state legislatures and sent to the president.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting in Mexico City in 2024.

item.title: Frank Hayden, Who Led Global Growth of the Special Olympics, Dies at 96
item.description: His research showing the positive effects of athletics on intellectually disabled children led Eunice Kennedy Shriver to ask him to help stage the Games.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Trump’s Boat Strikes Have Failed to Curb Cocaine Flow to U.S., Experts Say
item.description: Despite the rising body count off the South American coast, researchers say cocaine is as easy to get in many parts of the United States as it was before the strikes began.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A screen grab from a video posted by U.S. Southern Command this month showed what the U.S. military said was a vessel on a known narco-trafficking route in the eastern Pacific Ocean.

item.title: After New Push by the Bolsonaros, U.S. Labels Brazilian Gangs as Terrorist Groups
item.description: Following through on threats, the Trump administration has designated Brazil’s two largest drug gangs as terrorist groups.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Mauro Pimentel/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Red Command being arrested last year in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. designated the gang a terrorist group on Thursday.

item.title: Totó la Momposina, Colombian Folk Music Star, Dies at 85
item.description: Timbaland, Jay-Z and Major Lazer sampled her work, and she received a lifetime achievement award from the Latin Grammys in 2013.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Goodman
item.category: Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences
item.media:credit: C Brandon/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Totó in 2015. “We want to help our country’s music express the culture of all our people,” she said.

item.title: Carney Says That Canada’s Distancing is Good for U.S. in New York Speech
item.description: Speaking in New York, the Canadian prime minister called for a new relationship between Canada and United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Angela Weiss/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada speaking in New York on Thursday.

item.title: Cuba Loses Its Chance at Fuel After Russian Tanker Changes Route
item.description: A Russian tanker seemingly en route to Cuba with a lifeline of fuel has changed direction, a painful development for the island suffering under a U.S. oil blockade.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:credit: Jorge Luis Banos/Associated Press
item.media:description: Yankiel Perez carried buckets of water on Monday outside a building in Havana.

item.title: Guatemala Agrees to Joint Drug Gang Strikes With U.S., Expanding Trump’s Campaign
item.description: The deal is part of a broader effort by the Trump administration to press Latin American countries to agree to joint operations inside their borders.
item.dc:creator: Maria Abi-Habib and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security forces outside a prison in Guatemala City after a wave of riots and gang violence in January.

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:credit: Matias Baglietto/Reuters
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.
